title: Machine-Readable Governance Definitions
purpose: Introduce a Governance Definition Layer in JSON with machine-readable semantics. Markdown becomes a generated reflection of canonical JSON, not the source of truth. This makes governance enforceable, composable, evolvable, and validatable.
architectural_constraints: JSON definitions are canonical; markdown is generated
Narrative content (rationale, philosophy) is stored separately from operational semantics
Generation pipeline must produce deterministic output
CI must block merge if generated artifacts are stale

deliverables.description: JSON Schema for protocol definitions (states, transitions, rules, invariants, triggers)
Canonical state machine definition for execution lifecycle
Canonical trigger catalog and payload schema for checkpoints
Canonical lock rules and permitted mutation events
Generation script: JSON defs → markdown reflections + runtime validators
Generated TypeScript validator for execution lifecycle transitions
Generated TypeScript validator for checkpoint rules
Generated TypeScript validator for state mutation rules

acceptance_criteria: meta/governance/ directory has protocols/, schemas/, narratives/, recipes/, templates/
3 JSON protocol definitions validate against protocol-definition.schema.json
npm run governance:generate produces deterministic output
npm run governance:diff exits 0 (no stale generated files)
Generated markdown contains auto-generated footer citing canonical JSON source

failure_modes.scenario: JSON schema is too rigid and cannot express new protocol concepts

failure_modes.mitigation: Sort keys, pin timestamps, validate in CI

metadata.operational_rationale: Markdown-only governance is unenforceable. Without machine-readable semantics, protocols cannot be validated, composed, or evolved safely. This was observed when multiple documents defined overlapping recovery behavior with no resolution mechanism.
